summary:
The Chagatai Khanate (c. 1226–1347 AD, with successor states lasting longer) was the central khanate of the fragmented [[event:91|Mongol Empire]], inheriting the Central Asian heartland — modern Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, and Xinjiang — from Chagatai Khan, second son of [[event:2225|Genghis Khan]] and inheritor of the Mongol steppe homeland. Straddling the Silk Road's most critical middle section, the khanate was the geographic link between the [[event:2305|Golden Horde]] to the northwest, the [[event:2306|Ilkhanate]] to the southwest, and the [[event:2229|Yuan Dynasty]] to the east, making it central to the trans-Eurasian commercial connectivity of the Pax Mongolica.

The Chagatai Khanate was characterized by tension between the nomadic Mongol-Turkic pastoral tradition of the steppe north and the sedentary Iranian urban civilization of the oasis cities (Samarkand, Bukhara, Kashgar) to the south. This division eventually split the khanate: the western half (Transoxiana) became increasingly Islamized and urbanized, while the eastern half (Moghulistan) retained more nomadic Mongol characteristics. [[event:2276|Timur (Tamerlane)]] rose to power in the Chagatai successor state of Transoxiana in the 1360s, making Samarkand his capital and using the Chagatai political tradition as the foundation of his empire. Timur and his successors (the Timurid dynasty) synthesized Persian cultural sophistication with Mongol-Turkic military power in ways that directly influenced the Mughal Empire founded by Timur's descendant Babur in India (1526), making the Chagatai Khanate's legacy one of the most important in early modern Eurasian history.
